metadata.title: Structural Basis of NVX-0228 Selectivity for BRD4

chunks.content: The molecular basis of BD1 selectivity for BET bromodomain inhibitors remains poorly understood. Here, we report crystal structures of NVX-0228 (MW = 487.3 g/mol) bound to BRD4-BD1 at 1.8 Å resolution and to BRD4-BD2 at 2.1 Å resolution, revealing an unexpected allosteric mechanism of selectivity. Rather than acting as a classical competitive inhibitor of acetyl-lysine binding, NVX-0228 induces a conformational change in the ZA loop of BD1 that collapses the acetyl-lysine binding pocket, effectively functioning as an allosteric modulator. This mechanism is structurally incompatible with BD2 due to a key sequence difference at position 370 (Asp in BD1 vs. Glu in BD2).

BET bromodomain proteins each contain two tandem bromodomains (BD1 and BD2) that share approximately 75% sequence identity in their acetyl-lysine binding pockets. Developing BD1-selective inhibitors has been a major goal in the field, as selective inhibition may reduce on-target toxicities associated with BD2 engagement while preserving therapeutic efficacy. NVX-0228, a clinical-stage BRD4 inhibitor, was reported to exhibit approximately 50-fold BD1 selectivity, but the structural basis for this selectivity has not been elucidated. We undertook a comprehensive crystallographic and biophysical characterization to understand the molecular determinants of NVX-0228's selectivity profile.

Crystals of BRD4-BD1 (residues 44-168) in complex with NVX-0228 were obtained by co-crystallization in 0.1 M Bis-Tris pH 6.5, 25% PEG 3350, 0.2 M ammonium sulfate. Diffraction data were collected at the Advanced Photon Source beamline 24-ID-C and processed with XDS. The structure was solved by molecular replacement using PDB entry 3MXF as a search model and refined to 1.8 Å resolution (Rwork/Rfree = 0.182/0.215). The BRD4-BD2 complex structure was solved similarly and refined to 2.1 Å resolution (Rwork/Rfree = 0.195/0.238).

The 1.8 Å crystal structure of NVX-0228 bound to BRD4-BD1 revealed a striking and unexpected binding mode. Unlike other BET inhibitors such as JQ1 and I-BET762, which occupy the acetyl-lysine binding pocket in a canonical orientation, NVX-0228 binds at a site adjacent to but distinct from the canonical pocket. The triazolopyridazine core of NVX-0228 engages a hydrophobic groove formed between the ZA loop (residues 81-95) and the BC loop (residues 119-128). Binding at this site induces a 3.4 Å displacement of the ZA loop toward the acetyl-lysine pocket, effectively collapsing the pocket and precluding acetyl-lysine binding. This represents an allosteric mechanism of inhibition distinct from the competitive binding reported for other BET inhibitors.
chunks.section: Results - Crystal Structure of NVX-0228:BD1 Complex

chunks.content: Isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) measurements confirmed tight binding of NVX-0228 to BRD4-BD1 with a Kd of 2.3 nM (ΔH = -12.4 kcal/mol, -TΔS = 0.6 kcal/mol), indicating an enthalpically-driven interaction consistent with the extensive hydrogen bonding network observed in the crystal structure. The binding stoichiometry was 1:1 (n = 0.98 ± 0.05). Binding to BD2 was significantly weaker (Kd = 115 nM), corresponding to approximately 50-fold selectivity, in agreement with prior biochemical IC50 measurements.

The structural basis of BD1 selectivity was traced to a single residue difference: Asp145 in BD1 versus Glu460 in BD2. In the BD1 complex, Asp145 forms a critical hydrogen bond with the morpholine oxygen of NVX-0228 (distance = 2.7 Å). The longer side chain of Glu460 in BD2 introduces a steric clash with the morpholine ring, disfavoring the allosteric binding mode. Mutagenesis studies confirmed this: the BD1-D145E mutant showed a 38-fold reduction in NVX-0228 affinity, while the BD2-E460D mutant gained 22-fold in affinity.

Comparison of the NVX-0228 binding pose with those of established BET inhibitors highlights the unique nature of this interaction. While JQ1 (PDB: 3MXF), I-BET762 (PDB: 3P5O), and OTX-015 all bind within the acetyl-lysine pocket with the triazole ring mimicking the acetyl-lysine moiety, NVX-0228 is positioned 5.2 Å away from this canonical site. This unusual binding pose may explain the discrepancies in IC50 values reported across different assay formats, as assays utilizing acetyl-lysine displacement readouts may underestimate the potency of an allosteric inhibitor.

Our structural data fundamentally revise the understanding of NVX-0228's mechanism of action. Rather than a competitive inhibitor of acetyl-lysine binding as previously assumed, NVX-0228 functions as an allosteric modulator that induces conformational change in BD1. This distinction has important implications for resistance mechanisms: competitive inhibitors can be overcome by mutations in the acetyl-lysine pocket, whereas allosteric inhibitors may be resistant to such mutations but vulnerable to alterations in the ZA and BC loop regions. The allosteric mechanism also provides a rationale for the favorable selectivity profile and may guide the design of next-generation BD1-selective inhibitors.
